coredata.dc:title: Hierarchical ternary titanium dioxide decorated with graphene quantum dot/ZIF-8 nanocomposite for the photocatalytic degradation of doxycycline and dye using visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Rabeie, Bahareh
Mahmoodi, Niyaz Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the ternary nanostructure of titania (TiO2) decorated with graphene quantum dot (GQD)/zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) (T/G/Z: TiO2/GQD/ZIF-8) was synthesized by a hierarchical approach, and its photocatalytic activity was investigated for the degradation of the doxycycline (DOX) as a pharmaceutical and dyes under visible light irradiation. Increasing the doping content of the graphene quantum dot from 0.002 g to 0.02 g was accompanied by an increase in photocatalytic activity. A further increase to 0.2 g, the thickening or aggregation of the GQD layer on the TiO2 surface, or the thorough coverage of the TiO2 surface with the GQD occurred and effective light irradiation to TiO2 nanoparticles has not been done well. Thus the reduction of the catalytic activity happened. The photocatalytic degradation rate has increased outstandingly over this period of time for ternary composite in this concentration at intervals of the sampling period. The increase in pollutant concentration was associated with decreased photocatalytic degradation. Increasing the dose of photocatalysts improved the rate of pollutant degradation. The degradation efficiency of doxycycline in the presence of l-ascorbic acid scavenger had a notable decrease, while the presence of formic acid and isopropyl alcohol was less effective in the DOX degradation. It can be explained that the superoxide anion radical has the greatest effect on the degradation process of doxycycline. The DOX degradation kinetics followed the first-order kinetics with high correlation coefficients. This work has provided profound insight based on heterogeneous structures for efficient photocatalysts in the degradation of wastewater pollutants (pharmaceutical and dyes).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hierarchical ternary nanocomposite
TiO2/GQD/ZIF-8
Photocatalytic degradation
Pharmaceutical and dye
Visible light

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst otherkwds primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of graphene quantum dot content

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis approach of T/G/Z ternary composite.
FTIR spectrum of TiO2 (a), T/G binary nanocomposite (b), GQD (c), ZIF-8 (d), T/Z binary nanocomposite (e), and T/G/Z ternary nanocomposite (f).
Raman spectrums of T/G binary nanocomposite, T/Z binary nanocomposite, T/G/Z ternary nanocomposite, and ZIF-8.
XRD patterns of GQD, TiO2, MOF, as pristine materials, G/T and T/Z as binary nanocomposite, and G/T/Z ternary nanocomposite.
SEM images of TiO2 (a, b), T/G (c, d), ZIF-8 (e, f), T/Z (g, h, i), T /G/ Z (j, k, l).
EDX and MAP of (a) GQD, (b) G/T (c) G/T/Z.
N2 adsorption/desorption BET plot of ZIF-8 (a), T/Z (b), and T/G/Z ternary nanocomposite (c).
Investigation of the Optimum amount of GQD in binary composite T/G, in 100 mL of (250 mg/L) DOX solution with natural pH under visible light irradiation.
The DOX degradation by the synthesized binary (T/G, and T/Z), and ternary (T/G/Z) photocatalysts (100 mL of DOX solution (220 mg/L) and natural pH).
Effect of pharmaceutical concentration on DOX degradation by the T/G/Z ternary composite using visible light irradiation (9 mg catalyst, 100 mL of solution and natural pH).
Different dose of ternary T/G/Z nanocomposite, in 100 mL of (450 mg/L) DOX solution with natural pH, under visible light irradiation.
Evaluation of active species effect in the photocatalytic reaction using ternary T/G/Z nanocomposite (9 mg in 100 mL of DOX (450 mg/L) solution with natural pH).
Pollutant degradation kinetics: zero-order (a) TGZ, (b) TG, and (c)TZ, first-order (d) TGZ, (e) TG, and (f) TZ, and second-order (g) TGZ, (h) TG, and (i) TZ.
The schematic of photocatalytic degradation of doxycycline by ternary T/G/Z nanocomposite.
Photocatalytic degradation of various pollutants using TiO2-based composites.
BET data of pure MOF and the prepared nanocomposite.
Degradation kinetics constants of binary and ternary nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Hierarchical ternary titanium dioxide decorated with graphene quantum dot/ZIF-8 nanocomposite for the photocatalytic degradation of doxycycline and dye using visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Research, Institute for Color Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Color Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Research, Institute for Color Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the ternary nanostructure of titania (TiO2) decorated with graphene quantum dot (GQD)/zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) (T/G/Z: TiO2/GQD/ZIF-8) was synthesized by a hierarchical approach, and its photocatalytic activity was investigated for the degradation of the doxycycline (DOX) as a pharmaceutical and dyes under visible light irradiation. Increasing the doping content of the graphene quantum dot from 0.002 g to 0.02 g was accompanied by an increase in photocatalytic activity. A further increase to 0.2 g, the thickening or aggregation of the GQD layer on the TiO2 surface, or the thorough coverage of the TiO2 surface with the GQD occurred and effective light irradiation to TiO2 nanoparticles has not been done well. Thus the reduction of the catalytic activity happened. The photocatalytic degradation rate has increased outstandingly over this period of time for ternary composite in this concentration at intervals of the sampling period. The increase in pollutant concentration was associated with decreased photocatalytic degradation. Increasing the dose of photocatalysts improved the rate of pollutant degradation. The degradation efficiency of doxycycline in the presence of l-ascorbic acid scavenger had a notable decrease, while the presence of formic acid and isopropyl alcohol was less effective in the DOX degradation. It can be explained that the superoxide anion radical has the greatest effect on the degradation process of doxycycline. The DOX degradation kinetics followed the first-order kinetics with high correlation coefficients. This work has provided profound insight based on heterogeneous structures for efficient photocatalysts in the degradation of wastewater pollutants (pharmaceutical and dyes).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Following the increase in the population of cities, development of industries and increasing technology, effluents of textile and cosmetics factories, leather, printing, paper, rubber, and pharmaceutical industries, as well as hospital wastes, and general consumption of excess chemicals of aquaculture and animal processing, a serious and undeniable threat to human health and ecological balance create [1–5]. Owing to toxic metal complexes produced, teratogenicity, gene mutations, and related carcinogens, the development of resistance of pathogenic microorganisms to drugs, the indiscriminate use of antibiotics in animal husbandry and aquaculture breeding has become a growing crisis [6–8].

Many technologies, including chemical, physical, and biological techniques, have been utilized to treat wastewater from diverse industries, each with its own strengths and weaknesses. Photocatalytic methods are one of the most prominent subgroups of chemical methods for wastewater treatment that have recently been considered by many researchers. Not only can this method be more environmentally friendly and cost-effective than other methods, but it is also able to completely remove the contaminants from the environment by destroying their structure, this capability applies even to aromatic complex compounds. Other benefits of photocatalytic reactions include their effective application to self-cleaning surfaces as well as hydrogen gas production [9,10].

Despite its wide bandgap of TiO2 (~ 3.2 eV), non-toxicity, cost-effective production, ease of use, and good chemical and thermal stability, it led to being recognized as a popular semiconductor photocatalyst. So far, Great endeavors have been devoted to improving the surface by controlling the morphology of TiO2. Doping correction, as well as heterogeneous bonding, can be mentioned as the most common strategies in order to increase efficiency and expand the light adsorption region to visible light. Despite repeated studies, the expansion of the heterogeneous catalytic method of titanium compounds seems to be desirable for the photocatalytic decomposition of contaminants from contaminated water [11–14].

As a new subset of quantum dot (QD) materials, as well as the carbon family, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been the mainstay of much recent research. Specific physicochemical properties and a vast range of applications, for-instance light-emitting devices [15,16], solar cells [17,18], Bioimaging [19,20], adsorbent [21], photocatalysis [22] have made these materials more stunning than others. GQDs have been used in combination with titanium dioxide to improve their catalytic performance due to their extraordinary properties including tuned bandgap, high charge separation, and good mobility. The various applications of T/G (TiO2/GQD) composite photocatalytic degradation, solar cells, sensors, etc. have been investigated [23].

By Incorporation of GQDs and TiO2 particles, not only the wavelengths of light absorption can be extended, but also active sites created, and the electron-hole can be controlled The combination of TiO2 with GQD by creating excellent electrical conductivity, more surface area, structural flexibility, high thermal conductivity, mobility of charge carriers, chemical stability, and finally reducing the quantum-limited band gap, makes the resulting compound a promising candidate for various biomedical applications such as tissue engineering, bioassays, molecular imaging, and drug and gene transfer, as well as environmental remediation and solar fuel production [22].

Mineral-organic hybrids known as Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) are made of clusters or metal ions and multi-toothed organic bridges (ligands) that their large surface area, adjustable pore size and structure, with a plurality of active sites have transformed them into leading compounds in a variety of applications, including gas storage, gas separation [24,25], luminescence [26,27], catalyst [28,29], sensor [26], adsorption and proton conduction [30,31]. But their use as photocatalysts to reduce CO2, heavy metals, H2 evolution, and degradation of organic pollutants has been repeatedly considered by researchers. MOFs can be effective compounds compared to conventional semiconductors same as TiO2, ZnO, etc. [32–34].

The consensus on the capabilities of zeolites and MOFs leads to the emergence of a promising crystalline material called ZIFs. Actually, Zeolitic Imidazolate Frameworks (ZIFs), are a subclass of MOFs, consisting of tetrahedral metal cations including Zn2+, Co2+, and even Fe2+ bound by imidazolate linkers which owing to imidazole linker resembling bond angle to O-Si-O angle, they structured with a topology similar to zeolites [35,36]. It can be said that the rotation of imidazole rings is the reason for the characteristic flexibility of these frameworks [21,37,38] so that they are compounds prone to the effect of respiration (gate opening) and this has led to their multi-stage isotherms and has made these materials suitable for use in a wide range of effective applications [39,40]. High porosity, transition metal ions, unique surface area, tailored ligands as linkers, framework diversity, great stability, and large cavities can be characteristic of these compounds [41–45]. So far, TiO2 composites with ZIF-8 in the form of nanoparticles [46,47] or nanofiber [48] have been studied for various applications. ZIF-8 with special properties, thermal and chemical stability was considered an efficient component for designing with TiO2 composites [21,39].

The encapsulation of TiO2 nanoparticles for photocatalytic activity has been evaluated many times, but there was a possibility of instability in most cases. In fact, sustaining the photochemical and chemical constancy of the catalysts always subsisted as a challenge. ZIF-8 was a suitable choice as an encapsulator due to its thermal and chemical stability, which is characteristic of MOFs [47,49,50]. Striking improvement of TiO2 in photocatalyst activity by utilization of double active sites of GQD and ZIF-8 in ternary T/G/Z nanocomposite was observed.

The set of tetracycline antibiotics such as doxycycline (DOX) exists in the effluents of pharmaceuticals, animal husbandry, and aquaculture, and finally in surface water. Their high structural resistance and stability have caused the conventional wastewater treatment methods to have a slight effect on them, so research to remove these Materials can be beneficial and practical [51]. Table 1 and Fig. 1 present the properties and structure of doxycycline, respectively.

Thermal and chemical stability, crystallinity and specific surface area, and good photocatalytic activity have made heterogeneous semiconductor compounds suitable candidates for research and investigation by researchers (Table 2 ) [41,46,52–58].

In this work, a thin layer of GQD was placed on the TiO2 anatase nanostructure by etching, then the binary composite and zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 were incorporated and a ternary T/G/Z composite was formed. The photocatalytic activity of T/G/Z composite for the degradation of doxycycline under LED (light-emitting diode) light irradiation displayed the highest pollutant degradation efficiency compared to binary composites and TiO2 nanoparticles. This research has been done to improve the photocatalytic performance of pharmaceutical pollutant degradation using GQD and MOFs for TiO2 semiconductors.

Titania nanoparticles with 21 nm particle size and 15 m2/g surface area were obtained from Evonik Company. 2-methylimidazole organic ligand (99 %), and methanol (99.9 %) were purchased from Merck. The citric acid (99.5 %), and zinc nitrate (98 %) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich.
The synthesis method is similar to previous work [59,60].

TiO2 (0.1 g) was added to 10 mL of GQD solution (with different amounts of GQD (0.02, 0.2, and 0.002 g)), and stirred for 1 h at ambient conditions. After mixing, it was transferred to an autoclave and kept at 150 °C for 4 h. The resulting product was washed and dried at 80 °C for 12 h [53].

Initially, TiO2 nanoparticles (0.4 g) in 40 mL of methanol were sonicated for 22 min. Zn(NO3)2.6H2O (0.587 g) was added to the prepared solution and stirred for 15 min. In another container, a solution of 2-methyl-1H-imidazole (1.298 g) was added dropwise to the first solution, stirred, and rested for 24 h. The cream-colored precipitate was washed 3 times and dried in an oven at 80 °C [58].

First T/G nanoparticles (0.4 g) were sonicated in 40 mL of methanol for 22 min. Zn (NO3)2.6H2O (0.587 g) was added to the solution and stirred for 15 min. In another container, a solution of 2-methyl-1H-imidazole (1.298 g) in methanol was added dropwise to the first solution. The other steps are similar to the previous section) 2.2.3) (Fig. 2).

For FTIR spectroscopy, the mixture of sample powder and KBr were ground to soften condition, then pressed to produce a clear tablet. By placing the produced tablet in front of IR light, the resulting spectrum in the range of 4000 to 450 cm−1 was evaluated. SEM images were taken by LEO 1455VP to show the morphology and size of the samples. In this regard, materials were covered with gold to prepare for imaging. To inquire the crystal structure of the synthesized nanomaterials, the Siemens D-5000 XRD diffraction gauge (copper irradiation λ = 1.5406 A °) was utilized. Raman spectrometry (Takram P50C0R10 model Teksan spectrometer) was operated to investigate the features of synthesized samples. Microwave (SynthWAVE, Italy) was employed to the synthesis of graphene quantum dot nanomaterial. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller measurement was carried out by N2 adsorption/desorption using (ASAP-2020) micrometric surface and porosity analyzer. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer) was employed to measure the concentration of residual contaminant in the solution. For sediment collection of synthesized nanomaterials as well as separation of photocatalysts after the photocatalytic process, a Perkin Elmer Lambda-25 centrifuge was applied.

To evaluate the efficiency of the synthesized photocatalysts, pollutant degradation batch experiments were carried out using visible LED light irradiation at various doses of synthesized catalysts (binary (T/G, T/Z), and ternary composites (T/G/Z) composites), and different concentrations of the doxycycline. After preparing a solution with a specific concentration and adding a certain amount of photocatalyst, 100 mL of the pollutant solution was stirred for 90 min in the dark to achieve adsorption equilibrium. Then, 0.5 μL of H2O2 was added and the LED lamp was turned on. The solution samples were withdrawn every 30 min to study the photocatalytic degradation progress. After centrifugation, the residual amount of the DOX (and dyes) in the solution was evaluated with a dual-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer.

To evaluate the synthesized materials' functional groups, their FT-IR spectra were studied in the range 4000–450 cm−1 (Fig. 3). In the FTIR spectrums, the peak at 688 cm−1 has been attributed to the O-Ti-O stretching vibration. The presence of this peak in the structure of composites such as T/ G, T/Z, and T/G/Z can be the reason for their successful synthesis [58]. The significant peaks in T/G, and T/G/Z at 3432 cm−1 can indicate the stretching vibration of OH. The peak at 1630 cm−1 was attributed to the carbonyl group of GQD, and another peak at 1510 cm−1 was related to C-O-C vibration [21]. ZIF-8 spectrum fits well with previous reports. CH stretching vibrations have appeared in the range of 2850–3100 cm−1.The peak at 1540 cm−1 can belong to the CN in the ZIF-8 linker (imidazole rings). Besides, off-plane bending of the H-MIM caused peaks at 755 and 689 cm−1. Peaks available from 1400 to1500 cm−1 are attributed to CC stretching vibrations. The wide peak at 3430 cm−1 has referred to the OH group, in the case of MOF, it may have appeared due to the presence of solvent molecules in MOF pores [59,60].

Molecular vibrations by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 4) can be one of the complementary methods to confirm the structure of materials by researchers. In order to achieve the Raman spectrum of pristine ZIF-8, synthesized binary composites (T/G and T/Z) and ternary composites (T/G/Z), these samples were irradiated with the laser at λ = 532 nm. Initially, Six various vibration modes in the Raman spectrum of T/G and TiO2 (P25) anatase including A1g (515.93 cm−1), B1g (399.33 cm−1), Eg (640.34 cm−1), Eg (144.25 cm−1), Eg (196.49 cm−1) in accordance with previous articles, are clearly recognizable [57]. The graphene quantum dots' G-band in the range of 1535 cm−1 appears, which corresponds to the E2g vibrations of their hexagonal lattice, and the D-band of them in the range of 1210 cm− demonstrates the defects in their surface functional groups. One of the factors influencing the Raman scattering of GQDs can be their size, in fact, the frequency of the G band increases with enhancing their size [61]. In the pristine ZIF-8 Raman spectrum, the most of peaks available, are attributed to the functional groups of MOF organic ligands (imidazole rings, and their methyl derivatives(. The peaks at 685.92, 1147.82 and 1461.92 cm−1 refer to the vibrations of the imidazole ring, and the peaks in the range of 290–3000 cm−1 are due to the CH vibrations of the methyl group and ring. Two distinct and strong peaks 1147.82 cm−1 (D band) and 1461.92 cm− (G band) assign to vibrations of the network carbon (sp2), and possible network defects. The result of the ID/IG ratio for ZIF-8 obtained 0.72 [21,62]. The T/Z and T/G/Z Raman spectra show a set of peaks related to pure constituents)ZIF-8, GQD, and Titania). As shown in Fig. 3, due to the higher intensity of Titan peaks than ZIF, more accuracy is required to detect ZIF peaks in its binary and ternary composites. Peaks at 514.76, 394.49, 641.67, 144.25, and 199.02 cm−1 refer to TiO2 in the structure of the binary composite. Also, for ternary composites, these peaks appear in 521.79, 398.12, 641.67, 144.25, and 196.49 cm−1. At 1148.74 cm−1 (D band) and 1461.88 cm−1 (G band) can be observed for T/G/Z nanocomposite, and its ID/IG ratio reached 0.79.

X-ray diffraction analysis was used to evaluate the crystal phase of the synthesized samples. The XRD pattern, titania nanoparticles, the synthesized nanomaterials, and their composites (TiO2, GQD, ZIF-8, T/G, T/Z, and T/G/Z) are presented in Fig. 5. In the XRD pattern of titania nanoparticles, the index peaks at 26°and 48° are accord with titania anatase [47–49]. ZIF-8 is also well synthesized with characteristic peaks at 2θ = 7.44° (011), 10.55° (002), 11.20° (112), 14.56° (022), 16.69° (013), 18.19° (222), 21.94° (114), 23.45° (233), 24.8° (224), 26.77° (134), 29.82° (044), 30.99° (244), and 31.58° (235), these plates confirm the formation of ZIF-8 [21,63–66]. Also, the presence of graphene quantum dots caused a slight expansion in the XRD pattern of titania nanoparticles in the T/G and T/G/Z composites.

The morphology and size of the nanomaterials can be investigated using SEM images (Fig. 6). Refers to ZIF-8, titania as pristine material, and T/G, T/Z, T/G/Z, as their nanocomposites. Titania has appeared as homogeneous similar sizes (<50 nm) nanoparticles (Fig. 6a, b). In the binary composite, titanium cohesion is detectable (6c, d). The cubic zeolite imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) is almost homogeneous and the average particle size was <150 nm (Fig. 6e,f) [21,65]. In T/Z binary nanocomposite, the placement of titanium is observed on ZIF-8 cubic crystal (g, h, i). Finally in T/G/Z ternary nanocomposite, the layered state of the structure is well recognizable, which can provide a determined reason for the successful synthesis of nanocomposites (Fig. 6j,k,l) [47,58].

Map and EDX analyses of the synthesized samples were displayed in Fig. 7. The data presented that Ti and Zn elements exist in ternary composites. It confirms that the ternary composite was successfully synthesized.

The results of the BET analysis provide useful information about N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, the specific surface area of the synthesized materials as well as the size of the cavities and their volume. BET surface area of pure ZIF-8, T/Z, and T/G/Z composite were 1269, 815, and 710 m2/g, respectively (Fig. 8). The specific surface area of the pristine ZIF-8 was more than the binary nanocomposite, which can be attributed to the closure of the MOF pores by TiO2. But compared to titania/ZIF-8 binary nanocomposite, a higher specific surface area has been seen in the presence of GQD in the T/G/Z ternary composite. Interestingly, the size of the ZIF-8 pore is smaller than synthesized composites, 2.54 < 3.4 < 2.6 belongs to ZIF-8, T/Z, and T/G/Z, respectively [58]. Pursuant to the classification of IUPAC adsorption, desorption curves, pure MOF, and its binary and ternary composites were in good conformation to the IV isotherm type and H3 hysterics type. In these mesoporous materials, IV isotherm can be accompanied by hysteresis due to capillary condensation. This phenomenon happens while the pore diameter outstrips a special critical width [67,68] (Table 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of graphene quantum dot content
ce:section.ce:para: Since pure TiO2 shows poor photocatalytic performance in the visible light range, a thin and dispersed layer of GQDs was embedded on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles during the hydrothermal process. T/G binary composite with different amounts (0.002, 0.02, and 0.2 g) of GQD on the TiO2 nanoparticles (which we will denote as GT-0.002, GT-0.02, and GT-0.2) were synthesized, and their photocatalytic ability was compared (Fig. 9). In fact, the heterogeneous junctions of T/G nanoparticles were evaluated and optimized using the experimental method in the photodegradation of doxycycline as a pharmaceutical contaminant. Increasing the doping content of the graphene quantum dot from 0.002 g to 0.02 g was accompanied by an increase in photocatalytic activity, while as a result of a further increase to 0.2 g, the thickening or aggregation of the GQD layer on the TiO2 surface, or the thorough coverage of the TiO2 surface with the GQD occurred, and effective light irradiation to TiO2 nanoparticles have not been done well and the reduction of the catalytic activity happened [53].

To compare the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized samples, binary (T/G, T/Z), and ternary composites (T/G/Z) were investigated in similar operating conditions under the LED light. Doxycycline was chosen as the pharmacological contaminant mode. The results were summarized in Fig. 10. Mentioning the results clearly indicates that in comparing binary, and ternary composites, the photocatalytic degradation rate has increased outstandingly over this period of time for ternary composite in this concentration at intervals of the sampling period.

Contaminant concentration is an important parameter to evaluate the photocatalytic degradation ability of different catalysts. Thus, it was investigated in this research. In order to inquire about the effect of pollutant concentration on DOX degradation by T/G/Z ternary composite, different doxycycline concentrations (220, 350, 400, and 450 mg/L) were used at a constant H2O2 amount, pH of DOX solution, light irradiation time, and photocatalyst dose (Fig. 11). As expected, the increase in pollutant concentration was associated with decreased photocatalytic degradation. This event can be justified from two views. From one perspective, the number of pollutant molecules will be higher than the amount of the photocatalyst irradiated. Therefore the number of active radicals including superoxide (O2 ●-) and hydroxyl radicals (OH●) will not be commensurate with the amount of the pollutant. So, the reduction of DOX destruction occurs. From another point of view, as the concentration of pollutants increases, the penetration of photons into the DOX solution decreased. Therefore, by reducing the probability of light hitting the surface of the photocatalyst, the production of electron-hole pairs is also brought down. As a result, the production of active radicals is diminished and the possibility of their reaction with contaminant molecules and degradation of them is reduced [69]. The results (Fig. 11) indicated that the synthesized composite had a high degradation ability for high DOX concentration values.

In order to achieve the effect of photocatalyst dose on the degradation of doxycycline, different amounts of synthesized catalysts were evaluated by keeping other influencing factors constant (Fig. 12). In fact, increasing the dose of photocatalysts improved the rate of pollutant degradation [70]. As the dose of photocatalysts increases, their surface access and their active sites also increase, and the absorption of photons by the catalyst enhances. The result of this process is the emergence of more electron-hole pairs and the production of more active radicals and the upward growth of pollutant degradation.

Within the photocatalytic process, active species are created and one or a group runs the progress of this process. In order to determine the effect of the produced species in the degradation of doxycycline, by the T/G/Z ternary composite synthesized in this research, the trapping of hydroxyl radical (OH●), superoxide anion radical (O2 ●-), and hole (h+) species respectively by isopropyl alcohol, l-ascorbic acid and formic acid (FA) were evaluated (Fig. 13). 1 M of the mentioned scavengers was added to the solution of 450 mg/L pollutant with natural pH under the visible LED light. The results of the related test are presented in the figure. The degradation efficiency of doxycycline in the presence of l-ascorbic acid scavenger had a notable decrease, while the presence of formic acid and IPA was less effective in the DOX degradation. In fact, it can be explained that superoxide anion radical has the greatest effect on the degradation process of doxycycline.

To achieve the pollution degradation kinetics by optimal binary and ternary nanocomposites synthesized in different doses of photocatalyst load were evaluated in detail. Then concentration charts (C) vs. time (zero-order), C/C0 vs. time (first-order), and (1/C)-(1/C0) vs. time (second-order) were drawn. In Table 4 , the results of this research are presented. The photodegradation kinetics of DOX as a pharmacological pollutant were evaluated. The photodegradation of two binary composites (T/G and T/Z) and their ternary composite (T/G/Z) were studied to attain this aim. The photodegradation reaction was done using 100 mL of (450 mg/L) DOX solution at the natural pH with 0.50 μL of H2O2, under LED lamp irradiation. The acquired experimental data were analyzed using three kinetics models (Fig. 14). The DOX degradation kinetics followed the first-order kinetics with high correlation coefficients. The ternary composite shows the highest activity and best performance in DOX degradation compared to the other two samples. This confirms that incorporation of the design of titanium dioxide decorated with GQD/ZIF-8 ternary nanocomposite improves the effective photocatalytic activity of pure TiO2, and has created an efficient semiconductor-MOF composite.

The oxygen vacancy in TiO2 induces the light response in visible light irradiation. GQD possesses a relatively low catalytic efficiency under visible light irradiation, which can be related to their relatively high exciton binding energy. With the heterogeneity of TiO2, a sensitized T/G binary composite was obtained, which increased the response of the composite to visible light radiation. In addition, compared to TiO2, the T/G composite adsorbs more oxygen on the surface and produces more oxygen radical species, so it has a greater ability to degrade pharmaceutical pollutants. Irradiation of light on the photocatalyst motivates the initiation of a series of redox reactions and the excited electrons are transferred from the VB to the CB band and electron-hole pairs are appeared. In redox reactions, holes cause oxidation processes, and electron reduction processes on the surface of the catalyst. Electrons transferred to CB interact with O2 and O2 ●- will be created. OH● will be formed from the reaction of photo holes in VB and water. Finally, the h+ and electrons created after irradiation create O2 ●- and OH●, which cause pollutant degradation [53,71]. Since ZIF is more negative than titania, electron transfer from ZIF-8 to TiO2, as well as hole transfer from TiO2 to ZIF-8, is possible [55]. This heterogeneity formation in T/G/Z ternary composite increases electron-hole production by photons and improves photocatalytic activity by forming more radical species. Similarly, electrons transferred to TiO2 generate O2 ●- by reaction with atmospheric O2, and holes in the metal-organic framework react with atmospheric moisture to generate OH●. Finally, these generated reactive radicals act as oxidizing agents in the degradation of doxycycline. In fact, since combining TiO2 with MOF and GQD, the band gap is beneficially reduced and electrons and holes from other composite components can be transferred to TiO2, the photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite in the visible range has improved favorably [55,57].

According to the scavenger test to determine the dominant active species in the photocatalyst process, O2 ●- plays the principal role in the removal of DOX. The schematic of photocatalytic degradation of doxycycline by ternary T/G/Z nanocomposite is drawn in Fig. 15.

Several materials were synthesized and used to remove pollutants from water [72–82]. The regeneration of used materials is an important parameter in water treatment. Herein, the regeneration ability of the catalyst (T/G/Z ternary composite) was investigated. The results presented that the regeneration ability of the catalyst after three cycles did not decrease significantly. Regeneration ability remains above 98 % after the three cycles (100, 99, and 98 %). Thus, the synthesized catalyst had good regeneration ability.

The T/G/Z ternary composite was used as a catalyst for the degradation of dyes. The data indicated that dye degradation for Direct Red 23 and Direct Red 80 was 60 and 50 %, respectively. Thus, the synthesized composite could be used as an alternative photocatalyst for the degradation of the pharmaceutical and dye.

The main focus of this research is designing the T/G/Z ternary composite and evaluating its efficiency in the photocatalytic process for the degradation of pharmaceutical pollutants using LED (visible light) radiation. In fact, by taking advantage of the quantum and optical capabilities of graphene quantum dots and the unique structure of ZIFs, a new more stable ternary photocatalyst with more active sites, more suitable band gap, more electron transfer capability, and finally more significant pollutant degradation capacity, in the comparison of pure TiO2 was created. First, a binary T/G composite was synthesized by adding the graphene quantum dot) synthesized by the bottom-up method (to titanium dioxide by placing the compound under hydrothermal conditions. Then, a new ternary composite, T/G/Z, was prepared in a facile manner under ambient conditions. In the first performance study, pollutant degradation of T/G binary composites with different GQD content was compared for optimization. Then, the performance of the ternary composite was studied in detail for the pharmaceutical pollutant doxycycline. The research showed that the mentioned ternary composite followed zero-order kinetics and O2 ●- radicals were the dominant active species in this photocatalytic degradation process.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
